questions.question: Explain the difference between shared locks and exclusive locks in file sharing. Give an example scenario where each would be used.
keyPoints: Shared locks (read locks) allow multiple processes to read simultaneously
Exclusive locks (write locks) prevent any other access while held
Shared locks are used when multiple processes only need to read file data
Exclusive locks are needed when at least one process must modify the file
Operating systems typically allow shared locks to coexist but exclusive locks are mutually exclusive

questions.question: What is a race condition in file sharing? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Race condition occurs when multiple processes access/modify file concurrently and outcome depends on timing
Prevention methods include file locking (advisory or mandatory)
Atomic operations ensure indivisible read-modify-write sequences
Semaphores and mutexes provide synchronization
Proper use of OS-provided file locking mechanisms prevents race conditions

questions.question: Differentiate between UNIX semantics and session semantics in file sharing.
keyPoints: UNIX semantics: each read sees results of all previous writes - immediate visibility
Session semantics: changes visible only to process that made them until file is closed
UNIX semantics provides stronger consistency guarantees
Session semantics allows each process to work with its own view of the file
Most modern systems use variations of UNIX semantics for better data integrity

questions.question: Explain the readers-writers problem in the context of file sharing. What is the solution approach?
keyPoints: Readers-writers problem manages concurrent access to shared files
Multiple readers can access simultaneously but writers need exclusive access
Solution uses reader and writer locks
Reader lock increments a counter to track active readers
Writer lock ensures no readers or other writers are active before granting access

questions.question: What is the difference between mandatory and advisory file locking? Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Mandatory locking is enforced by the OS - cannot be bypassed
Advisory locking requires processes to voluntarily check and respect locks
Advisory is more flexible and widely used in UNIX systems
Mandatory provides stronger guarantees but less flexible
Advisory can lead to data corruption if processes ignore locks
Mandatory ensures data integrity even with uncooperative processes

questions.question: What is a file lease? How does it help in managing file sharing in distributed systems?
keyPoints: File lease is a time-limited lock granted to a client process
If lease expires without renewal, OS automatically releases it
Prevents stale locks when client crashes or becomes unresponsive
Useful in distributed systems where network failures can leave locks dangling
Lease renewal mechanism allows continued access while process is active

questions.question: Explain the concept of atomic file operations. Why are they important in file sharing?
keyPoints: Atomic operations complete entirely or not at all - no partial states visible
Important for maintaining file integrity during concurrent access
Examples include atomic rename, atomic write operations
Prevents inconsistent file states when multiple processes modify file
Implemented using file locking and transaction mechanisms at OS level

questions.question: Discuss the various mechanisms available for implementing file sharing in operating systems. Explain how each mechanism ensures data consistency and prevents concurrent access problems.
keyPoints: File locking mechanisms: shared locks, exclusive locks, mandatory vs advisory
Semaphores and mutexes for synchronization
Atomic operations for indivisible read-modify-write
Session semantics vs UNIX semantics
File leasing for distributed systems
Transaction-based approaches for ACID properties
Cache coherence in distributed file systems
Deadlock prevention and detection in file sharing
Each mechanism addresses specific concurrency problems with trade-offs between complexity, performance, and data integrity

questions.question: Explain the concept of concurrent access control in file systems. Discuss the problems that can arise without proper synchronization and the solutions provided by modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Problems without synchronization: race conditions, data corruption, inconsistent file states, lost updates
Modern OS solutions include multi-level locking (coarse-grained and fine-grained)
File system level locks vs application level locks
Kernel-mediated vs user-mediated locking
Use of fcntl() and flock() in UNIX for file locking
Windows mandatory locking vs UNIX advisory locking approaches
Distributed file systems face additional challenges with caching and replication
Modern solutions incorporate lease-based mechanisms and optimistic concurrency control

questions.question: In distributed file systems, file sharing becomes more complex due to multiple clients and network latency. Discuss the challenges and solutions for file sharing in distributed environments.
keyPoints: Cache coherence challenges when clients cache file data locally
Network partitioning can cause split-brain scenarios
Lease mechanisms help manage cache consistency
Optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency control
Version vectors track file modifications across clients
State machine replication for consistent file updates
Compromises between consistency, availability, and partition tolerance (CAP theorem)
Modern systems use eventual consistency with conflict resolution
Examples: NFS, CIFS/SMB, Andrew File System (AFS) approaches

questions.question: Design a file locking mechanism for a multi-user database system. Your design should handle both read and write operations efficiently while preventing data corruption and deadlock situations.
keyPoints: Multi-granularity locking: database level, table level, record level
Shared locks for read operations allowing concurrent readers
Exclusive locks for write operations blocking all other access
Two-phase locking protocol to prevent deadlock
Lock escalation from fine to coarse granularity when needed
Deadlock detection using wait-for graph
Lock timeout and retry mechanisms
Write-ahead logging for recovery
Implementation using semaphores and condition variables
Performance considerations: minimize lock contention while ensuring data integrity
